segments.title: Correcting a D-class length error
segments.summary: A follow-up corrects the D-class length figures and shows why even familiar reference books need a second check.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by acknowledging readers' corrections to dimensions used in the earlier D-class discussion. He had initially treated the disagreement as a question of particular subclasses because the reference work was one he had used successfully before. Returning to the figures turns the short correction into a practical historical-method lesson: a plausible number still needs an independent check of its units and conversion.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke introduces the correction, describes reader feedback, and explains why he returned to the sources.

segments.title: Checking feet-to-metre conversions
segments.summary: The reference book's feet measurements are sound, but several printed metric conversions are not, demonstrating how a trusted source can still introduce error.
segments.body: The episode pinpoints the problem in the metric conversion rather than the underlying imperial measurements. Dr. Clarke notes that the same book had been reliable for other cruiser classes, which made the error easier to miss. He accepts responsibility for relying on that record without recalculating the figures himself, despite having the necessary feet values in front of him.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies correct feet figures alongside faulty metre conversions and explains why the error was not caught earlier.

segments.title: Recalculating the cruiser lengths
segments.summary: A live recalculation revises the comparative lengths used in the earlier episode, including one figure that had been overstated by more than ten metres.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke works through the conversion again and replaces the earlier length comparison with the recalculated result. The difference is large enough to alter the way the ships should be compared, rather than being a harmless rounding issue. It is a useful reminder that dimensions are often the starting point for claims about a ship's performance, so an error can travel into later analysis.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke recalculates the disputed figures, gives revised lengths, and states that one comparison differed by about eleven metres.

segments.title: Hull form, power, and the speed question
segments.summary: The revised dimensions raise a design question: similar weight and power do not make speed predictable when hull proportions and propellers differ.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke outlines a useful rule of thumb that, for the same horsepower, a longer and narrower hull tends to gain speed. He immediately treats it as an opening for investigation rather than a complete explanation, because the compared cruisers' speed figures do not line up neatly with the corrected dimensions. Propeller form is the next variable he identifies for a closer look.

evidence.note: The transcript connects length, beam, weight, horsepower, and speed before identifying propeller shape as a further question.

segments.title: Why corrections belong in teaching
segments.summary: The closing reflection turns reader feedback and a public correction into a lesson in careful historical method.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke thanks the viewers who raised the issue and stresses that their feedback was both observant and constructive. He plans to use the episode as an example for students: lecturers can make errors, and correcting them requires care rather than defensiveness. The lesson extends beyond cruiser dimensions to the habit of tracing figures to their source and checking them before building an argument on them.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke thanks the commenters and says the correction will be used as a teaching example about checking work.
